does: This paper conducts a systematic analysis of AI evaluations as a governance mechanism for catastrophic risk prevention, examining both their capabilities and fundamental limitations. The authors demonstrate that evaluations can establish lower bounds on AI capabilities and assess certain misuse risks when evaluators invest sufficient effort, but they identify critical failure modes inherent to the evaluation paradigm itself. The paper presents concrete evidence that evaluations cannot establish upper bounds on capabilities, reliably predict how future models will behave, or adequately assess risks from autonomous AI systems. Through this analysis, the authors argue that while evaluations remain valuable as one component of safety governance, they should not serve as a primary mechanism for ensuring AI safety, and they outline incremental improvements to frontier AI safety practices that acknowledge these constraints.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on AI governance and safety evaluation frameworks that has emerged as frontier models have grown more capable. It responds to an implicit assumption in current AI policy and safety case development that technical evaluations can comprehensively establish safety bounds before deployment. The paper engages with prior work on capability assessment, misuse risk evaluation, and autonomous AI systems research, while connecting to broader governance discussions about what verification and validation can achieve in complex technological domains. It extends earlier critiques of purely capability-testing approaches by systematizing the theoretical and practical limitations of the evaluation paradigm, drawing on insights from adjacent fields about the challenges of bounding unknowns in rapidly evolving systems.
enables: This analysis creates space for alternative or complementary approaches to AI safety governance beyond evaluation-based assurance cases. By clearly delineating what evaluations can accomplish, the paper enables more realistic policy design that combines evaluations with other mechanisms such as robust control systems, incentive alignment, monitoring frameworks, and structural governance approaches. The work points toward research directions for developing better predictive frameworks for model capabilities, understanding autonomous system risks more systematically, and creating safety cases that rely less heavily on exhaustive pre-deployment testing. It also motivates deeper investigation into governance approaches borrowed from other high-stakes domains and supports arguments for ongoing monitoring and adaptation rather than one-time safety certification, opening pathways for more dynamic and evolving safety frameworks.
